segments.title: Insect class China and Tigris origins
segments.summary: The Insect class is introduced as the Royal Navy's large China gunboat type, with roots in river campaigns and imperial presence work.
segments.body: This watch point explains why the Insect class should be read as a river and station vessel rather than a light cruiser despite its six-inch guns. Dr. Clarke links the class to the Fly-class small China gunboats, the Tigris River campaign, and earlier lessons about moving goods and firepower along rivers. The segment gives the basic operating logic: a navy needed shallow-draft armed ships where inland waterways, treaty-port crises, and expeditionary campaigns made larger warships irrelevant. Firefly's Ottoman capture and recapture by Tarantula also previews how directly these small craft could participate in contested river operations.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the Insect class the large China gunboats and places the Fly class beside them as small China or Tigris gunboats.
The transcript ties the ships to the Tigris and Mesopotamia campaigns, river movement of goods, and gunboat presence in China.

segments.title: Treaty-exempt gunboat design fit
segments.summary: The class fits the treaty-era small-ship category: under 2,000 tons, slow, torpedo-free, lightly protected, and modified as threats changed.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the Insect class to show why small gunboats sat outside the headline treaty debates. Their displacement, speed, and lack of torpedoes kept them in the same broad exemption space as sloops, even though their six-inch guns gave them real local punch. The design tradeoff is plain: little or no formal armor, improvised protection where possible, and later anti-aircraft additions for ships that were expected to work close to shore rather than fight in a battle line. Their poor rough-water suitability was the other side of the shallow-draft design, constraining deployments while making close river and coastal access possible.

evidence.note: The transcript says gunboats fit with sloops because they are under 2,000 tons, below 20 knots, and carry no torpedoes.
The transcript describes wartime weapon changes, lack of armor, improvised protection, and 1939 replacement of some six-inch guns.
The transcript describes distant-station presence, close-inshore access, and the ships being unsuitable for rough seas.

segments.title: Mesopotamia mobile fire support
segments.summary: The Mesopotamia campaign shows why Insect and Fly gunboats mattered: they gave mobile firepower and river access that land artillery could not provide.
segments.body: This segment moves from the 1914 Basra landing and halted advance to the moment when gunboats made further river movement more practical. Dr. Clarke stresses that the advance toward Kut and Baghdad depended on Insect and Fly-class gunboats for fire support. The key lesson is operational rather than decorative: in river campaigns, a gunboat could move with the front and bring guns to bear where towed artillery could not keep pace. Accuracy while under way was imperfect, but forts and shore batteries were usually stationary targets, so mobility still gave the flotilla a decisive practical advantage.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the initial Mesopotamia campaign, the Basra landing, and the difficulty of moving north toward Baghdad.
The transcript says the Insect and Fly-class gunboats supported river movement and that advances depended on their mobile fire support.
The transcript summarizes their fire-support purpose and caveats their accuracy while moving.

segments.title: HMS Aphis from the Danube to Mediterranean fire support
segments.summary: HMS Aphis's battle honours trace how a First World War river gunboat remained useful across the Mediterranean in the Second World War.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses HMS Aphis to show the Insect class's unusually long operational reach. Built by Ailsa Shipbuilding, she served on the Danube in 1917 and spent much of her later career on the China Station. Her Second World War honours extended across Libya, Sicily, the Adriatic, and southern France. The sequence makes Aphis a compact example of a specialist river craft repeatedly repurposed for coastal bombardment and inshore support far from its original theatre.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Aphis's builder, Danube service in 1917, and long China Station career.
The transcript lists Aphis's Second World War honours in Libya, Sicily, the Adriatic, and southern France.

segments.title: HMS Bee and the cost of replacing proven gunboats
segments.summary: The sale of HMS Bee and loss of her newer replacement, HMS Scorpion, sharpen the video's argument about interwar small-ship procurement.
segments.body: HMS Bee was sold for scrap at Shanghai in March 1939 after HMS Scorpion replaced her. Scorpion represented the newer Dragonfly-class generation, but she was lost to Japanese surface forces in February 1942. Dr. Clarke explicitly acknowledges hindsight while arguing that bringing Bee home would have preserved another useful inshore hull. The episode shows why one-for-one replacement can reduce resilience when war quickly removes the new ship and the discarded predecessor is no longer available.

evidence.note: The transcript says Bee was sold for scrap in Shanghai after replacement by the newly built gunboat HMS Scorpion.
The transcript identifies Scorpion's loss to Japanese cruiser and destroyer forces in February 1942.
The transcript gives Bee's March 1939 sale and argues that returning her home would have been useful, while marking the judgment as hindsight.

segments.title: Wanhsien seizures and gunboat coercion
segments.summary: The opening phase of the 1926 Wanhsien incident shows HMS Cockchafer using armed presence to reverse seizures of British merchant ships.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins the Wanhsien incident with troops under General Yang Sen seizing the British merchant ship SS Wanhsien. HMS Cockchafer's nearby six-inch guns gave her boarding party coercive leverage, and the soldiers released the vessel after a heated confrontation. A later collision involving SS Wanliu killed Chinese soldiers and led to another attempted ship seizure, which Cockchafer again interrupted. The watch point shows how river gunboats protected commercial access and diplomatic claims, but also how local power struggles, commercial pressure, and accidental violence could rapidly escalate the mission.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces HMS Cockchafer in the 1926 Wanhsien incident and describes the seizure of a British merchant ship and the gunboat-backed intervention.
The transcript discusses the seizure campaign as a possible effort by General Yang to extract more resources from Western trading companies.
The transcript describes the Wanliu collision, loss of Chinese soldiers and materiel, and Cockchafer's intervention against another attempted seizure.

segments.title: Wanhsien rescue attempt becomes a firefight
segments.summary: A camouflaged merchant ship, naval boarding party, and covering gunboats turn the Wanhsien hostage rescue into a costly river battle.
segments.body: After General Yang's forces captured British officers and concentrated artillery ashore, negotiations failed and the Royal Navy attempted a forced rescue. The British camouflaged and armored SS Kiawo, crewed her with sailors from Cockchafer, Despatch, Scarab, Mantis, and other gunboats, and sent her alongside the seized vessels. The boarding party recovered the hostages under fire while Widgeon and Cockchafer exchanged fire with Chinese troops, suffering officer casualties before withdrawing. Dr. Clarke uses the outcome to expose the limit of gunboat diplomacy: the hostages were saved, but the ships remained behind, Chinese soldiers and civilians were killed, property was destroyed, and coercive presence had become open combat.

evidence.note: The transcript describes General Yang's escalation, failed negotiations, and the Royal Navy's conversion and manning of SS Kiawo for the rescue attempt.
The transcript follows Kiawo's approach, the boarding under fire, hostage recovery, covering gunfire, British casualties, and withdrawal.
The transcript frames the action as the moment gunboat diplomacy's bluff was called and describes the destructive aftermath and pressure on General Yang.

segments.title: HMS Cricket's Mediterranean loss and surviving wreck
segments.summary: HMS Cricket's final service traces an old river gunboat from China to Mediterranean inshore duty, air attack, and a surviving wreck off Cyprus.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke follows HMS Cricket from the Mesopotamia campaign and Russian Civil War to China and then the Mediterranean Inshore Squadron, where Italian aircraft crippled her in 1941. Declared a constructive total loss, she was stripped for spares and her hulk was later used as an RAF target off Cyprus. The surviving wreck gives the class a tangible archaeological afterlife and shows how vulnerable these shallow-draft gunboats became once exposed to modern air power. Cricket's path also demonstrates how long the Royal Navy kept reassigning these specialist hulls whenever sheltered-water duties still matched their limits.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Cricket's transfer from China to the Mediterranean, her crippling by Italian air attack, and her declaration as a constructive total loss.
The transcript says Cricket's hulk was towed to Cyprus, used for RAF target training, and remains accessible to divers.

segments.title: Panay and Yangtze crisis risk
segments.summary: British Insect-class ships around the Panay incident show how river gunboats sat inside 1930s China crisis politics.
segments.body: The Panay section connects British and American river patrol work on the Yangtze during the Second Sino-Japanese War. Dr. Clarke notes that Ladybird and Bee were attacked as well, helped pick up survivors, and operated in a crowded information environment with journalists and foreign-owned vessels close to the fighting. News cameras, correspondents, visible national flags, and attacks on boats carrying wounded survivors made the incident exceptionally difficult to contain or obscure. The watch point helps explain why gunboats were not just patrol craft: they were diplomatic signals, rescue platforms, press access, and potential flashpoints in the same narrow river space.

evidence.note: The transcript says Ladybird and Bee were also attacked, reached the scene to pick up survivors, and that Japan accepted responsibility and apologized.
The transcript describes the attack on USS Panay, machine-gunning of small boats, and the American flag painted on the ship.
The transcript explains why journalists and other observers used American or British gunboats to get close to fighting while retaining some protection.

segments.title: HMS Tarantula becomes a support ship and fleet flagship
segments.summary: HMS Tarantula's conversion from river combatant to repair and office ship culminated in service as Admiral Bruce Fraser's early British Pacific Fleet flagship.
segments.body: HMS Tarantula had recaptured Firefly from the Ottoman Empire and supported fighting on the route to Baghdad during the First World War. By the Second World War she had become a repair, support, and office ship at Trincomalee. That unglamorous capacity made her useful to Admiral Bruce Fraser, who used her as his British Pacific Fleet flagship in 1944 before larger headquarters space was available. Her career shows force multiplication in another form: an old gunboat hull could release scarce frontline ships from static command and support duties.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Tarantula in repair, support, and office service and says Admiral Bruce Fraser used her as his British Pacific Fleet flagship in 1944.
The transcript contrasts Tarantula's earlier river combat with her later flagship form and notes her sinking as a gunnery target in 1946.

segments.title: HMS Moth is scuttled, recovered, and lost in Japanese service
segments.summary: HMS Moth's recovery after the fall of Singapore shows how denying a damaged vessel could fail and return a gunboat to war under a new flag.
segments.body: HMS Moth was under repair when Singapore fell, and the British scuttled her to prevent capture. Japanese forces nevertheless recovered the gunboat and returned her to service as Suma. She survived until March 1945, when a mine sank her with the loss of eight crewmen. The sequence makes scuttling a concrete wartime logistics problem: a wreck was only denied to the enemy if damage, time, and local salvage capacity made recovery impractical.

evidence.note: The transcript says Moth was under repair at Singapore, was scuttled when the city fell, and was then recovered by Japan.
The transcript says the recovered gunboat struck a mine in March 1945 and sank with eight crewmen lost.

segments.title: HMS Ladybird supports Bardia, Kastellorizo, and Tobruk
segments.summary: Ladybird's Mediterranean service demonstrates the class's value in bombardment and amphibious support despite age, low speed, and air vulnerability.
segments.body: HMS Ladybird had to be stripped and towed from Singapore toward the Mediterranean because the old shallow-draft hull was poorly suited to an ocean crossing. Damage during the journey limited her to seven knots, yet she bombarded Bardia during Operation Compass and landed Royal Marines during the attempt to seize Kastellorizo. She later supported Tobruk by shelling Gazala airfield and ferrying supplies until dive bombers set her on fire in shallow water. Because the wreck remained above the surface, her three-inch gun continued serving as an anti-aircraft weapon, extracting one last defensive use from a lost ship.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Ladybird's stripped-down tow from Singapore, hull damage, seven-knot limit, and resulting Port Said guard duty.
The transcript covers bombardment of Bardia, the Kastellorizo landing, Tobruk support, and Ladybird's crippling by dive bombers.
The transcript says Ladybird's three-inch gun continued in use as an anti-aircraft weapon because the wreck remained above water.

segments.title: HMS Gnat at Nanking and the next gunboat generation
segments.summary: HMS Gnat's Nanking service and relief by HMS Grasshopper connect interwar protection duties with the Royal Navy's newer Dragonfly-class gunboats.
segments.body: The Nanking incident shows HMS Gnat performing the protective side of gunboat diplomacy by helping safeguard British and other international civilians during unrest. Her later relief by HMS Grasshopper introduces the Dragonfly class, evidence that the Royal Navy still saw a need for purpose-built river gunboats in the late 1930s. The six newer boats carried four-inch guns and continued the same close-water presence mission with a more modern design. The transition links the Insect class's imperial station work to the next generation of small inshore vessels rather than treating the type as a First World War dead end.

evidence.note: The transcript says Gnat participated in the Nanking incident and helped protect British and other international citizens in China.
The transcript says Gnat was relieved by HMS Grasshopper and identifies the six newer Dragonfly-class gunboats and their four-inch armament.

segments.title: HMS Gnat survives U-79 off Tobruk
segments.summary: Gnat's survival after a U-79 torpedo hit illustrates both the durability and the adaptable afterlife of an old inshore gunboat.
segments.body: HMS Gnat joined destroyers and the monitor Terror in supporting the 6th Australian Division's assault into Tobruk. U-79 torpedoed her in October 1941, but the small gunboat remained afloat and was towed to Alexandria. Although declared a constructive total loss, she served for years as a fixed anti-aircraft platform before scrapping in 1945. Dr. Clarke closes the example by stressing why the class mattered: shallow draft let its six-inch guns move close to landings and deliver powerful, relatively accurate fire against shore targets.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Gnat's support for the Australian assault into Tobruk, her torpedoing by U-79, tow to Alexandria, and constructive total loss.
The transcript emphasizes Gnat's subsequent fixed anti-aircraft service and the surprising survival of the torpedo hit.
The transcript summarizes the class's close-inshore fire-support value during troop landings.

segments.title: Gunboats as force multipliers
segments.summary: The closing argument treats gunboats and sloops as treaty-light force multipliers that could have freed destroyers for fleet work.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the scrapping of older Insect-class ships and the late Dragonfly-class program to make a procurement argument. He argues that treaty limits did not prevent Britain from building useful gunboats and sloops in greater numbers before the Second World War. A steady peacetime program could also have produced better-developed small ships than the cheap emergency types rushed into service during wartime. The broader force-structure lesson is that small, unglamorous vessels could take on inshore, river, and anti-submarine tasks, freeing destroyers and larger combatants for missions only they could perform.

evidence.note: The transcript says some Insect-class ships had been scrapped and argues treaties still allowed categories such as sloops and gunboats to be built in useful numbers.
The transcript discusses late Dragonfly-class construction and losses, showing how more small ships might have mattered.
The transcript argues for regular peacetime gunboat and sloop construction, freeing destroyers and treating small ships as force multipliers.
